concept: API (Application Programming Interface)
analogy: An API is like a waiter in a restaurant. You don't go into the kitchen yourself — the waiter takes your order, brings it to the kitchen, and returns with your food. Similarly, an API takes your request to a system and brings back the response, without you needing to know how the kitchen works.

concept: API (Application Programming Interface)
analogy: An API is like a recipe card that only lists what ingredients you need to provide and what dish you'll get back. You don't need to know the chef's techniques — just follow the interface.

concept: API (Application Programming Interface)
analogy: An API is like the scoreboard at a stadium. It gives you the information you need (score, time, stats) without requiring you to understand how the timing systems, sensors, and databases behind it work.

concept: Recursion
analogy: Recursion is like standing between two mirrors facing each other. Each mirror reflects the other, creating an infinite series of reflections — each one a smaller version of the same image. Recursion works the same way: a function calls itself, each time with a slightly smaller problem, until it hits a base case (like the mirrors getting too small to see).

concept: Recursion
analogy: Recursion is like a recipe that says 'to make lasagna, make a smaller lasagna and put another layer on top.' You keep making smaller lasagnas until you reach the base case: a single layer. Then you build back up.

concept: Recursion
analogy: Recursion is like a fern leaf. If you look at a single branch of a fern, it looks like a miniature version of the whole fern. Each sub-branch repeats the same pattern at a smaller scale — that's recursion in nature.

concept: Recursion
analogy: Recursion is like a tournament bracket. To find the champion, you need the winner of two semi-finals. To find each semi-final winner, you need the winners of quarter-finals. You keep breaking it down until you reach individual matches — the base case.

concept: Cache
analogy: A cache is like keeping your most-used kitchen tools on the counter instead of in a deep drawer. It takes longer to dig through the drawer, so you keep the things you reach for most often within arm's reach.

concept: Cache
analogy: A cache is like a mise en place — pre-measured, pre-chopped ingredients laid out before cooking. Instead of going back to the fridge and cutting board every time, everything you need is already prepared and at hand.

concept: Cache
analogy: A cache is like a quarterback's pre-snap read. Instead of analyzing every defender after the snap, they memorize key formations beforehand. When they see a familiar setup, they already know where to throw — no need to recalculate.

concept: Encryption
analogy: Encryption is like sending a letter in a locked box. Anyone can see the box being delivered, but only the person with the right key can open it and read the letter inside.

concept: Encryption
analogy: Encryption is like writing a song in a made-up language. Anyone can hear the melody, but only someone who knows the language can understand the lyrics. The 'key' is knowledge of that language.

concept: Encryption
analogy: Encryption is like a secret family recipe where the ingredients are written in code. 'Add 3 cups of sunshine' might mean '3 cups of lemon juice.' Without the decoder, no one can replicate the dish.

concept: Database index
analogy: A database index is like the index at the back of a textbook. Instead of reading every page to find where 'photosynthesis' is mentioned, you look it up in the index and jump straight to the right pages.

concept: Database index
analogy: A database index is like labeling every drawer and shelf in a warehouse. Without labels, workers search every shelf. With them, they go straight to the right spot. The labels cost a little space and upkeep, but massively speed up retrieval.

concept: Load balancing
analogy: Load balancing is like a grocery store opening new checkout lanes when lines get long. Instead of everyone waiting in one lane, customers are distributed across multiple lanes so everyone gets served faster.

concept: Load balancing
analogy: Load balancing is like a basketball coach distributing minutes across players. If one player plays the entire game, they'll burn out. By rotating players in and out, the team maintains performance throughout the game.

concept: Load balancing
analogy: Load balancing is like a head chef assigning dishes across multiple line cooks. If one cook gets all the orders, the kitchen slows down. By distributing orders evenly, food comes out faster and more reliably.

concept: Version control
analogy: Version control is like Google Docs' version history, but much more powerful. You can see every change ever made, who made it, go back to any previous version, and even create parallel 'what if' versions of the document without affecting the original.

concept: Version control
analogy: Version control is like keeping every revision of architectural blueprints. If a new design causes structural problems, you can pull out the previous revision and see exactly what changed. You can even have two teams working on different blueprint variations simultaneously.

concept: Machine learning model training
analogy: Training a machine learning model is like learning to cook by taste-testing. You make a dish, taste it (evaluate), adjust the seasoning (update parameters), and try again. Over hundreds of iterations, you learn what combination of ingredients produces the best result — without anyone giving you the exact recipe.

concept: Machine learning model training
analogy: Training a model is like a basketball player practicing free throws. Each shot gives feedback — too far left, too short. Over thousands of shots, they adjust their form until they consistently score. The player doesn't memorize each exact shot; they develop an intuition (a model) for the right motion.

concept: Machine learning model training
analogy: Training a model is like learning to play guitar by ear. You hear a note, try to match it, hear the difference, and adjust your fingers. Over time, you develop the ability to play songs you've never heard before — you've learned the patterns, not just memorized specific songs.

concept: TCP/IP handshake
analogy: A TCP handshake is like a phone call. You dial (SYN), the other person says 'Hello?' (SYN-ACK), and you say 'Hi, it's me' (ACK). Only after this three-step exchange do you start the actual conversation. If any step fails, you hang up and try again.

concept: TCP/IP handshake
analogy: A TCP handshake is like the pre-game coin toss ritual. The referee flips the coin (SYN), one captain calls it (SYN-ACK), and the referee confirms the result (ACK). Only after this agreed-upon exchange does the game begin.

concept: DNS (Domain Name System)
analogy: DNS is like the contacts app on your phone. You don't memorize your friend's phone number (192.168.1.1) — you just tap their name (google.com) and your phone looks up the number for you. DNS does the same thing for the internet.

concept: DNS (Domain Name System)
analogy: DNS is like a building directory in a lobby. You don't need to know that Acme Corp is on floor 14, suite 1402. You look up the company name in the directory and it tells you where to go. DNS translates human-friendly website names into the actual server addresses.

concept: Containerization (Docker)
analogy: A container is like a shipping container. Before shipping containers, loading a ship was chaotic — every item was a different shape and size. Shipping containers standardized everything: pack your goods in a standard box, and it works on any ship, truck, or train. Docker does the same for software — package your app with everything it needs, and it runs the same everywhere.

concept: Containerization (Docker)
analogy: A container is like a meal kit delivery box. Instead of hoping the chef's kitchen has the right ingredients and tools, the meal kit comes with everything pre-measured and included. No matter whose kitchen it lands in, the result is the same dish.

concept: Git branching
analogy: Git branching is like a tree growing new branches. The trunk is your main code. When you want to try something new, you grow a branch off to the side. If the branch bears fruit (the feature works), you graft it back onto the trunk. If not, you prune it — the trunk is unaffected.

concept: Git branching
analogy: Git branching is like splitting a batch of soup into two pots. You experiment with new spices in one pot while keeping the original untouched. If the experiment tastes great, you use that recipe going forward. If not, you still have the original.

concept: Overfitting in machine learning
analogy: Overfitting is like a tennis player who only practices against one opponent. They learn every quirk and habit of that opponent and dominate them — but lose to everyone else. They memorized one opponent instead of learning general tennis skills.

concept: Overfitting in machine learning
analogy: Overfitting is like memorizing one specific recipe so precisely that you measure ingredients to the milligram — but you can't improvise or adapt when an ingredient is missing. You learned the recipe, not how to cook.

concept: Overfitting in machine learning
analogy: Overfitting is like a musician who can play one song perfectly note-for-note but can't jam or improvise. They memorized the recording instead of learning the underlying music theory.

concept: Middleware
analogy: Middleware is like airport security between the check-in desk and the gate. Every passenger (request) has to pass through it. Security doesn't handle flights or check-ins — it just inspects, validates, and either lets you through or stops you. Middleware sits between the request and your application, doing similar inspection and processing.

concept: Middleware
analogy: Middleware is like a food taster in a royal kitchen. Every dish passes through the taster between the chef and the king. The taster doesn't cook or eat the meal — they just check it and pass it along (or reject it). Middleware inspects and processes requests before they reach your main application.

concept: Rate limiting
analogy: Rate limiting is like a bouncer at a club who only lets in 10 people per minute. Even if 100 people show up at once, the bouncer controls the flow to prevent overcrowding. If you've already entered too many times tonight, you're told to come back later.

concept: Rate limiting
analogy: Rate limiting is like a dam on a river. The river might surge after heavy rain, but the dam controls how much water flows downstream at any given time, preventing floods. Rate limiting does the same for server requests.

concept: some physical traits able to skip through a generation
analogy: You have two copies of each gene, one set from your father, one from your mother. Each copy is called an _allele_. Alleles can be recessive or dominant. If you have one dominant allele from one parent and one recessive from the other, the dominant allele will be expressed. Only if you have two recessive alleles will that version of the gene be expressed. So, it's easy for some traits to skip a generation if they're recessive, since there are more ways for a dominant allele to take effect. Let's say you have the a recessive big nose gene. The dominant allele is N and the recessive one is n. Mother - N, father - N = NN, a small nose Mother - N, father - N = Nn, a small nose Mother - n, father - N = nN, a small nose Mother - n, father - n = nn, big nose

concept: The significance of logarithms in Time Complexity (of computer algorithms)
analogy: Mathematically, if log(n) = x, that means that 10^x = n. It's related to other powers as well, so you can also use log(n) to describe something like 2^x.  This comes up a lot for binary searches, where you find a number on a sorted list, by checking the middle number and cutting the list in half based on whether the number you are looking for is greater or less than the middle number. Let's say you have a list of 16 numbers. You will need to check 4 numbers to complete the search. First check cuts the list down to 8, second one down to 4, third one down to 2 and fourth one down to 1. If you double the size of the list, that only requires one extra check. If x is the number of numbers to check and n is the size of the list, you can say 2^x = n, which you can rearrange into log n.

concept: Can someone ELI5 Fourier Series and Transforms
analogy: This may be LI4, but: Say you want to make 11, but you only have a 4s and 1s. You would take two 4s and three 1s and add them together, right. Now say you have a crazy function (curvy line on a graph), that's your "11". It turns out that you can take a bunch of sine and cosine functions (these are your 4s and 1s), and add them together just like we did with the 4s and 1s to get your curvy line. A little more complex: constructing a given function (your curvy line) takes a precise combination of the right sine and cosine functions. And just like we took **two** 4s and **three** 1s, we add those sines and cosines with different *weights* or *coefficients*. Say the given function is more like sin(x) than cos(x), we will add a large sin(x) to a small cos(x).

concept: Kirchoff's current law
analogy: **Current law**: Amount of current entering a junction (the place where two or more wires meet) is the same as the amount of current leaving the junction.  **Analogy**: Think of a busy traffic intersection where many roads meet. On any given day, the total number of cars that have arrived at the intersection will be equal to the total number of cars that have left the intersection. **Volatge law**: Amount of voltage being generated in a closed network (a bunch of wires forming a loop) is the same as the amount of voltage being lost by all the components in the network.  **Analogy**: Imagine a closed circuit as a waterslide in which you end up where you started. Now, the total amount of upslope that you climb will be equal to the total amount of downslope that you slide.

concept: practically every PSX/PS1 games appears to have problems with the depth/z-buffer
analogy: The PS1 didn't *have* a depth-buffer. It was essentially all software-rendered, so it was up to the developers to deal with it. I imagine on such basic hardware that the bandwidth and memory cost of implementing it would be too much. 3D games probably used something similar to the BSP polygon-sorting or scanline culling approach also used in Quake, Wolfenstein, etc to implement hidden surface removal.

concept: - Hyperbaric welding / Underwater Welding - How does the welder not get shocked
analogy: Electric current flows from areas of high potential to areas of low potential via the path of least resistance. In this case, you've got an area of high potential (the electrode) and an area of low potential (the thing you're welding), and the path of least resistance is the place where those two things touch. The path of least resistance is *not* through the water around those things … just like when the guys on *The A Team* weld in a shed somewhere, the path of least resistance isn't through the air around them. In other words, it's the same basic reason why you can hold a cup of water with a hole in the bottom, and the water won't climb up the sides of your cup and get your hand wet. It's only going to flow through the path of least resistance, which is out the hole on the bottom.

concept: the ball bearing, when crushed by the hydraulic press, give out so many sparks
analogy: Steel burns, but is usually either not hot enough, or in too big a bit for the oxygen to get at. Crushing it adds a lot of energy, so it gets very hot but it's also brittle, so it shatters. The tiny hot fragments are very similar to the particles thrown off when grinding, and burn in the same way.

concept: white plastic yellow over time? Is it true for all white plastic
analogy: UV light and oxygen can both cause plastic to yellow over time. I'm not sure how UV light changes it, though I imagine it's similar to sun burn. Oxygen is a very reactive chemical and causes a lot of things to change over time (plastics, iron, food).

concept: the OS know what RAM is in use by which programs
analogy: Imagine I'm the owner of a security deposit vault. You want to store some stuff in my vault, so you ask if I have a couple of spare boxes. I check my records and find that yes, I have some spare boxes. I tell you which ones they are, and update my records to show that those boxes belong to you. I also make sure you don't use any boxes which aren't yours. And I keep my records in one of the boxes. In this analogy, the vault is RAM, and each box is a chunk of memory. I am the operating system, using some of my RAM to keep track of which bit of RAM is owned by each program. And you are a program, asking me for a bit of RAM and then using what I allocate to you.

concept: Why can't cancerous tumours be simply cut out
analogy: It was explained to me sort of like this in hospital: It's like dropping ink into water, if you get there quick enough, the cancer is just in one place, and you can scoop it out. But when the cancer spreads, that ink diffuses through the water, and it's virtually impossible to get every trace of it out. Your only option at that point is to attack the whole thing and hope you can kill it all. It's not a perfect analogy, obviously, and even those that get there quick enough to have the tumor simply be removed will possibly need chemo/radiation to kill cells around the effected area, but it helped me to understand how if you have colon cancer that has spread to the liver, or lungs, or both, that just 'removing the cancer' is near impossible.

concept: History channel still labeling sci-fi shows "documentary" even after Discovery C
analogy: I would imagine it's similar to what happens to MMO's when they change the balance too much. Your old user-base moves on and you get stuck with whoever didn't mind the change. If you try to change back or appeal to the old group again it generally makes more people leave instead of join so you are stuck in a cycle of pandering to who's left. With the Discovery Channel, they used to be known as a great source of solid information. Once they started going soft, all the people interested in real info left to find a new source and then the only financially beneficial types of shows are the ones catering to the conspiracy theorists or reality show addicts.

concept: there a stereotype in movies/games/books that if someone/something is huge it ca
analogy: Because its true. Its like a car. A small car can turn within a smaller circle and do it quicker. Imagine in a roundabout. A truck on the other hand has to do it slower. Why? Because its bigger. When something is big, it takes more work to make it change direction. This gets harder the larger you are. A bicycle is small and can change its direction with very low force. A car is bigger and needs more power. A truck needs an even greater amount. An airplane needs a ton of it.

concept: an engine idling at 3K rpms sound like it will explode soon
analogy: Lets make an analogy. If you lift a hundred pounds, your arms generate a certain amount of force, right? It takes a lot of energy to lift that weight. Now imagine using the same amount of force-however many joules of energy that was- and just wildly flailing your arms. Youd injure yourself flailing around. Simply put, an engine is doing the same thing-all that power isnt being usefully directed, its just flailing around causing issues.

concept: file compression work? (.rar, .zip)
analogy: Ok, say you have some file whose content is (way simplified) AAAAAAAABBBBBBBBCCCCCCCCDDDDDDDD. This takes 32 characters to represent. Compression works by compressing it into 8A8B8C8D. This takes only 8 characters to represent. This is an incredibly simplified compression algorithm though. Most work by replacing common strings (lets say "the") with one character like 'a'. They must also append a decompression dictionary (because the most common word in one file isn't the same as another) which tells winzip/winrar how to decompress the file. This is why you can never get a file to be smaller than a certain size, no matter how much you compress it.

concept: What happens when I power off or unplug my computer while it's installing an upd
analogy: I like to think of it like this: Lets say you are fixing a roof on a house (you are the only one that can do this), right after you tear off the roof, but before you you start rebuilding the roof, you get shot. Damn no one can use that house, as you are the only one that could fix the roof. * "You" -- Update Program * "house" -- The computer * "roof" -- A Program or other thing being updated with in the computer (OS or otherwise) * "get shot" -- Power turned off If there is luck involved, you might turn into a zombie and finish the roof. Thus the house can be used again. But most of the time you are in the ground, and the house has no roof. * "Zombie" -- Update program restarting itself upon reboot.

concept: you have to send a ZIP/rar file through the internet instead of sending a folder
analogy: Because a folder isn't a thing. The only thing that exists on your hard drive is files. What is sent back and forth on the internet is files.  All a folder is, is a file structure marker. When you open lets say the pictures folder, the hard drive scans all the files the picture folder points to and displays those ignoring all the rest.

concept: What states of matter are marshmallows, flesh, bread and Jell-O? Why
analogy: They are all solids (or, because they're so complex, *mostly made up of solids with some moisture*), because they do not change shape to fill out a container they're in.  Let's say you have a substance, X. You put X in a jar with a cover.  If X just sits there and doesn't change shape, it's a solid. If X spreads out along the bottom, it's a liquid. If X expands to fill up the entire container, it's a gas. Marshmallows, flesh, bread, and Jello would just sit there, not changing shape, therefore they're all solids--or items that are comprised mostly of solid but a large amount of liquid.

concept: a photonic boom
analogy: Light traveling through some medium must always move at the speed of light through that medium. But particles are not constrained by this rule. Just like when something moves faster than the speed of sound, there's a sonic boom. When a particle moves through some medium faster than the speed of light though that medium, there is [Cherenkov radiation](_URL_0_). Some people call it a "photonic boom," but it's not a common term.

concept: Why I get significantly less dizzy when spinning if I snap my focus between two
analogy: It does have to do with your inner ear. Imagine a large tall wine glass. if you continually swirl it eventually you can really get the liquid going. so much that it will ride way up the glass and when you stop will continue to swirl for along time.  Now swirl that glass just once quickly. yes the liquid will slosh but settle quickly.  Similar to your inner ear, if you continually swirl it you build up energy that doesn't immediately dissipate when you stop (making you dizzy). but snapping our head to fixed positions breaks up the continuous motion and helps keep the energy in the liquid down.

concept: Why scientists look for earth-like conditions to declare whether or not a planet
analogy: We have one instance of life that we know of. Can there be other types of life? Maybe, even probably.  However, we don't *know* that. It's like looking at a box and saying "is that a computer?" If there's no input or output, probably not. However, you could build a tablet with wireless charging and no ports, so it's possible. With the screen off it would just look like a box.  The point is - science is expensive. Generally they're not saying its uninhabitable for *all life,* just life as we know it, and that's the only type of life we know about, so the rest is speculation. Just like AskScience, real life science is pretty strict about speculation.

concept: old-school gameboy screens/calculators/digital watches display a picture without
analogy: Paintings produce a picture without producing any light. They rely on ambient light around them for you to see it. a Gameboy's screen is an LCD just like your phone. Instead of a backlight in the background it uses a reflective surface that the ambient light bounces off of. Both screens have a film over them with a liquid crystal matrix on it, when electricity passes through a cell in the LCD matrix it changes from opaque to transparent or the other way around.

concept: Why don't I end up at the back of the airplane if I jump straight up in the air
analogy: Inertia.  Because you are travelling *with* the plane, you inherit its speed/momentum. Just like when you sit in a car, all the passengers get carried along with it. So when you jump, you continue to travel at the same speed.  If you could somehow do really, really, really long jumps, then you might slow down marginally (because you're not receiving the continual engine thrust that the rest of the plane is), but not sufficiently for you to end up all the way at the back.

concept: in words like "Prophet", why use "ph" instead of "f". Profet
analogy: Most words with a ph in English have Greek origin as the letter 'phi' (φ). In ancient Greek, phi was pronounced pretty much with how you'd imagine a ph would sound - a "p" with a strong outward breath, similar to the letter p at the beginning of "**p**aint". This was rendered in the Latin alphabet (which English uses) as "ph". But since that time, its usage in both Greek and Latin shifted to something similar to the modern "f", and it's that pronunciation that was picked up by English. But since we took the word from Latin, we kept the Latin spelling as 'ph'. TL;DR: Spelling changed more slowly than pronunciation did, as is typical in a society that has written language but not sound recordings.

concept: I only download picture messages
analogy: picture messages use a technology called MMS, multimedia messaging system. It is similar to SMS, short messaging system, better known as texting.  MMS and SMS are part of the mobile phone standard. They work with the way that the cell phone tower talks to the phone. They go through a system that is owned by the phone service companies. They don't use the internet so they can't go through wifi. Just in case you or someone else reading this doesn't realize, wifi is a standard that lets you connect to the internet which is a system of connected computers. It is a public system that thousands of different companies have connections too.

concept: What's the biological explanation behind aging
analogy: If someone has a better answer, I'll delete...but...your DNA is constantly copying and dividing over and over and over. With each copy, you lose a little bit on the end (telomeres - junk DNA.) Each copy, you end up with a little less than you started with. Then there are a million other literal variables like mutation, disease, and the like that can interfere and mess things up a bit. In a sense, you can almost think of it like having an original copy and photocopying it. It's a pretty close representation...mostly indistinguishable. Then you photocopy the copy. Then you photocopy the copy you made from the copy...and, once you do that enough times...it looks a lot different than the original.

concept: If dolphins and whales are mammals who don't have gills and need to frequently c
analogy: Imagine an astronaut having spent one year in space. Despite all the exercise to maintain his strength in a zero-G environment, his heart will have shrunk by two third, his bone mass will have reduced dramatically and its muscles will have followed the same path. Back on Earth, the astronaut will be carried out of the capsule straight onto a wheelchair, and it will take weeks before he can get back on his feet. Life in the sea is like living in zero-g. But now, consider these mammals having spent their WHOLE LIFE in that environment, following millions of years of evolution inside that environment, without having trained their body to support their weight on land... You can now picture the disaster their body will face once on land.

concept: a menger sponge have no volume
analogy: Because you're talking in terms of infinities. As the surface area approaches infinity, the volume approaches zero. The point of the sponge is that you're removing mass from the sponge, so the volume wouldn't remain the same, for the same reason that if you took a cube of wood and drilled a hole in it, the volume would change. To continue the cube analogy, imagine repeatedly drilling holes through a block of wood in such a way that none of the holes intersect. Eventually you'd end up with paper thin walls which give you a lot of surface area, but not a lot of actual mass. Now continue that forever. And you can see how the surface area increases at the expense of volume.

concept: Why downloading is so much faster than uploading
analogy: The internet connection to your house has a limit to how much data it can transfer, called *bandwidth*. Bandwidth is measured in megabits per second (Mbps). So let's say you have a 10 Mbps connection. You could split it in half, and devote 5 Mbps for uploads and 5 Mbps for downloads. But most people download a *lot* more than they upload...it would be kind of a waste to be waiting through a big download with that 5 Mbps upload connection unused. So a lot of providers split the connection unevenly, say, 8 Mbps down and 2 Mbps up. You are getting 60% more download capacity, at the cost of slower uploads. For most people, that is a big win.

concept: Quantum physics experiments suggest that reality doesn't exist until it is measu
analogy: Observed does not mean by a sentient being. Observed simply means interaction in this context. Here's an analogy: Say you're measuring the temperature of water with a thermometer that starts off with a temperature, say 20C, and the water you're measuring has a temperature of 50C. If you're measuring a pool, it doesn't matter. If you're measuring a tiny droplet of water, the heat of the thermometer will effect the temperature of the water! It's like that for quantum particles. In order to measure them, we have to interact with them, which then collapses the waveform. Note that the Heisenberg uncertainty principle has nothing to do with this.

concept: If spacetime is expanding, are we also expanding
analogy: As i understand it, over short distances such as those in our bodies, the fundamental forces such as the "strong force" keep us together even as the universe expands. The frequent analogy for 'everywhere is expanding' is to imagine dots on an inflating balloon. Instead imagine the the earth and the sun (or you and me) are stickers on an expanding balloon. The space around you is expanding and pulling the stickers away from one another, but the forces holding the sticker together is likely strong enough to keep it from stretching or tearing.

concept: one speaker vibrate to play multiple sounds at the same time
analogy: I'll repeat an answer I gave earlier: Let's say we have two speakers that produce two different simple tones. What happens then? The two tones get mixed in together to make a more complicated sound. This can be hard to picture but if you look at [this image](_URL_0_) you can see a bit more easily how ups and downs mix together to give the more complicated shape. And if a speaker would makes the bottom soundwave instead of using two speakers you would experience the same sound.

concept: going on when my brain takes fifteen to twenty seconds to remember something
analogy: No filing cabinets... Ok, imagine each memory is a clearing at the end of branching paths through a forest. FOr memories you access often, you know the correct path without having to think about it, and you arrive at the memory without trouble. Sometimes, you may forget a path to a memory, and you have to spend some time backtracking through different paths, or maybe just standing there scratching your head. Best analogy I can think of without getting into brain anatomy.

concept: it that paper cups get mushy
analogy: Paper cups are coated with a waterproof layer on the inside, but not on the outside. When they contain hot drinks, the liquid is touching this impermeable barrier, so doesn't get the paper wet. However, a cold drink cools its container, making water vapour from the atmosphere condense on the outside - that's why your ice water glass "sweats" on a hot day: the water forming on the outside is not seeping through the glass, but rather condensing out of the air, just like when you breathe on a mirror and it fogs up.  However, the paper cup only has a waterproof barrier on the inside. This condensation on the outside of the cup can soak into the paper and make it go soggy.

concept: anti-aging products work (if they work at all)
analogy: A key to understanding this is that "anti-aging" is an incorrect label, it is not really what these products do. They are more like "anti-aging effects." They don't stop the actual aging process, they treat the superficial symptoms of aging. It's like having an old house with structural problems, water damage in the walls, and peeling paint on the outside. You can strip the paint off the outside and slap a new coat of paint on, and now it looks as new as the day it was made. But you have not stopped the aging process of the structure, and you have certainly not addressed any of the fundamental problems that will make the house unlivable soon. You only treated the surface. That is what "anti-aging" products do. They can't actually reverse the true effects of aging.

concept: tilting your cup to the side reduce the amount of head when pouring a beer
analogy: Think of it like a playground slide. If you just jumped off the top of it onto the ground, it would hurt (large impact, large amount of force). If you jumped down the slide portion and slid down, it would be more comfortable (less impact, less force). Just like with soda, there is CO2 in your beer. When you shake (cause impact to) the beer/soda, the CO2 is released. So by "sliding" the beer down, you put less impact/force on the beer, giving you less bubbles.

concept: What happens when you add salt to ice
analogy: The salt competes with the water to bond to water molecules. Normally, water molecules form loose hydrogen bonds where the positive side of the water sticks to the negative side of another molecule, making water "sticky", which means it has a relatively high freezing point. Since they already stick together very well, it takes more energy to melt it. Salt molecules are polar, just like water molecules. There's a positive side and a negative side to the salt molecule. So the salt also wants to stick to the water molecules in the same way. The salt gets in and sticks to the water, getting in the way of the water sticking to itself. That makes it easier to melt the water, lowering the melting point so water will stay liquid even at or below 0 degrees C.

concept: car wheels look like they're slowly moving backwards sometimes when a car is mov
analogy: It's called the wagon wheel effect, and it's because your visual processing system isn't realtime, it in fact snaps many photos per second. Imagine your neighbor drives down your street every day at 7pm. If you capture one video frame every day at 7pm, he'll appear to be in the same place for the whole video. Now imagine you start taking a picture at 7pm, then 6:59pm, then 6:58pm. He'll appear to be going *backwards* down your street, when in fact all that's happened is you've changed the frequency with which you take pictures.

concept: How exactly do exo suits work
analogy: Imagine a strong, hollow robot. You can control it with your thoughts or movements from the inside, but it's ultimately the one doing all the work. It's powered by electricity (or fusion, or combustion, or whatever) just like any robot would need to be, so its motors or hydraulics are stronger and faster than you are. Since you're piloting it from the inside, it's moving you around with it, functionally increasing your otherwise only-human capabilities. We don't have many powered exoskeletons on the market just yet, but there are a lot in development. I wonder how far they'll go!

concept: How to programmers
analogy: Here's an analogy: Imagine a game developer is a painter. They've just got brand new, state of the art brushes, paints and canvases. They're a bit different to what they're used to, but they still manage to make a painting that looks pretty good. In doing that painting, they've learnt a bit about their new tools. How they're different to the old ones, what they're good at, what they're not so good at. So knowing that, they do another painting, and it looks better. The tools haven't changed, but their ability to use them has improved. With game development there's an extra factor. The next game is built from the old one. So when making their next game, they have more time to improve what they already have instead of having to make it from scratch.

concept: Why can't I see in a mirror
analogy: When you say the image is all in the same plane, you're probably thinking of a mirror as being in some way similar to a television or computer monitor. That's not the case -- a monitor or television produces its own picture by **emitting** light, whereas a mirror is merely a device for **reflecting** light that originates somewhere else. That means that when you're looking at your wife's reflection when she's standing 6 feet beind the mirror, the light from her face is travelling all the way to the mirror. It then reflects off and travels a further couple of inches to your eyes. So in total the light has travelled 6 feet + a few inches, and as far as your eye's lens is concerned it's the same as looking at your wife standing that distance away.

concept: you only know an electron's position if you give up on knowing its momentum
analogy: Imagine you wanted to take a detailed and clear photo of a mosquito buzzing around your room. You'd have to zoom in really close to get such a picture. Imagine then that you've zoomed in far enough so as to be able to clearly see the mosquitoes features and decide to snap a photo. What do you see? A mosquito stuck in time. How can you possibly tell from that still picture where the mosquito will fly to next or even at what speed its flying at. The same is true for the opposite. To be able to see the mosquitoes movements and speed you'd have to zoom out, but then the mosquito becomes a small insect buzzing around the room and you've lost any sense of detail and picture quality.

concept: Why people cover their mouth with hand in scary, shock situations
analogy: I think the mouth-gape is a natural reaction, however, covering it with your hands is a cultural thing. It is considered not polite to leave your mouth open wide, so people cover it out of habit, just like when you're yawning or coughing.

concept: I assume that being allergic to gluten is an actual allergy people have been fac
analogy: Theoretically your tolerance for gluten is on a spectrum. You can compare it to lactose intolerance. Some people will have a full-on auto-immune response to milk. Others will just shit water. Others will just get acne or nasty farts.  In the same way, some people have Celiacs disease while others "just" have a gluten sensitivity. The argument that's driving the fad is that many people have a sensitivity to gluten but it's below a threshhold where they immediately realize it as such. Just like lactose intolerance.

concept: reaction time develop or improve
analogy: The same way any skill or ability does. Imagine a bucket with a hole in the bottom. If you pour a gallon of water into the bucket, it'll take a minute to drain. That's your average, lazy, unattuned, slow-reaction-time brain.  As you do things that require quick reactions, you start to poke more holes in the bottom of the bucket. It might have taken a full minute to drain a gallon of water with one hole, but with 10, it only takes a few seconds. That's what your brain and muscles do - they develop more(and more efficient) neural pathways which allow you to process inputs faster, and thus, react faster.

concept: What exactly are we smelling (breathing in) when we smell an odor/fragrance etc
analogy: Imagine inside your nose there are millions of tiny "slots" or "keyholes." Only certain types of molecules will fit just right. When a molecule fits, it registers a certain smell. There are only seven different types of "base" smells and every thing you smell is basically a mixture of these seven smells. Kind of similar to how you can make thousands of different colors just by mixing primary colors. In order to smell something it has to be chemically volatile, which simply means there is some kind of chemical breakdown spewing molecules into the air. When you're cooking, there is a large amount of heat (energy) involved which means the molecules which break free during the cooking process have a lot of speed and can travel farther than other smells.

concept: I've heard that Roller Coaster Tycoon is coded "in assembly"
analogy: Assembly language is the language the processor speaks directly. Typically when writing code, you use a higher level language. To use an analogy, if I want to "code" you to go buy groceries, I'll say "Go pick up groceries. There's a list on the counter". That would be the sort of language that computer programmers usually use. In assembly language, it would be something like "Go find the counter. Search for a piece of paper. Put the paper in your pocket. Grab your car keys. Get in the car. Start the engine. ..." Well, you get the idea. As you can probably imagine, coding in assembly is time consuming, difficult, and hard to maintain. But, you can sometimes optimize the way the computer works, making it faster than a higher level language.

concept: The relationship between heat and time
analogy: Heat flows from areas of high concentrations to areas of low concentrations and in controlled environments (e.g. no energy input) it never flows back, it's a law of thermodynamics. Heat continues to flow until it is evenly distributed throughout an entire system. While heat is flowing, we are able to assign a direction to the flow of time because heat flow a one-way street.  The way you can relate this to the pendulum analogy is to imagine that the energy you put into the pendulum to swing it can represent heat in a system. Over time, the pendulum will slow down due to air resistance, the energy you put into the pendulum spreads out into the environment. The amount of time that passes is directly proportional to the amount of energy that the pendulum has lost to the environment.

concept: machine-learning algorithms
analogy: At the most basic level, instead of telling a computer what to do, you give it the tools to learn from what it has done. For example, imagine a tic-tac-toe game. Instead of telling it what to do, you give it the ability to remember games; and whether or not it won the game. then, when it has to make a move, it looks at previous games, and sees which moves resulted in wins, in loses, and in ties; and picks the move that has the best result, on average. For more complicated problems, you make things work differently; but the same general idea is in effect: you set the computer up to keep track of what goes well, and what goes badly; and take action that is most likely to go well.

concept: phones and computers transfer audio to headphones
analogy: Sound is transmitted as a voltage signal through wires. The voltage changes just like the soundwave, and this goes into tiny magnets in headphones, or speakers, or telephones. The magnets shake the speaker back and forth as the voltage changes, and this movement makes pressure waves on the air. The pressure waves we hear as sound. The whole process works in reverse too, and that's how microphones work. This also means that if you look at a soundwave picture (or wave form), you are actually also looking at a [graph over time](_URL_0_) of the in and out movement of the speaker you're listening to.

concept: nuclear explosion create so much damage
analogy: The really small scale world works very differently than our human sized world, so don't feel bad if it doesn't seem to make intuitively sense. Atoms make up stuff, like Lego blocks make stuff. Except imagine that the Lego blocks are held together by rubber bands. If you break a large Lego structure, the rubber bands snap and it hurts your fingers a bit. Separating two blocks is relatively easy, but when you try to break a single block in half, it gets much more difficult. But we figured out we actually can do that, and it turns out that if you break a single Lego block, it's like the single block is being held together by a billion rubber bands, and when you snap them all at once, they hurt like a motherfuck.

concept: audio work with VCR tapes
analogy: The tape has several tracks that run along the length. One is for video, one for audio, one for control. [Here is a picture.](_URL_0_) The blue is the video, yellow are audio and control.  The audio itself works pretty much the same way as a cassette tape. An electromagnet is dragged along the tape, and the desired signal is played through the wire of the electromagnet. This imparts a magnetic signature into the tape. During playback you draw an electromagnet across the now magnetized tape, and it will induce a current with the same signal as was originally used to magnetize the tape.

concept: Goo Gone work
analogy: According to Goo-Gone's MSDS (material safety data sheet), it is about 95% hydrotreated light petroleum distillates. This is similar to liquid paraffin or naphthalene, both of which are extracted from crude oil via distillation, the same way we get gasoline from crude oil. It's a solvent, with a bit of citrus oil to make it smell nice.

concept: some pictures from the late 1950s through the late 1960s look so clear
analogy: There's been good cameras and shitty cameras forever. Old 1950s Professional Speed-Graphic cameras used absolutely huge negatives, so were pretty high quality, but cheap 35mm equipment also existed. Over time the trend in picture taking was to cheap and convenient (witness how many point and shoot 35mm and APS cameras there were a decade or two ago), not quality. Just like the trend from uncompressed CDs to MP3 files with lossy compression. The trend is still going on with the switch to cell phone cameras. Although they're getting better to the point that they're better than some cheap point and shoot cameras, right now they're not anywhere close to a thousand dollar DSLR.

concept: When two hydrogen atoms fuse the energy released come from mass
analogy: Since nobody has answered this yet, it's because of the [nuclear binding energy] (_URL_0_). Basically, a small component of the mass of each hydrogen atom is stored potential energy, and that potential energy is released when the atoms fuse into helium. Note that mass and energy are equivalent and related through e = mc^2 . It's like having two magnets come together. When they are apart, they have very slightly more mass than when they are together, because they have potential energy from being "high up" in a magnetic field. When the magnets come together, energy is released in the form of heat and sound. If you had a sensitive enough scale, you would notice the mass of the magnets to be slightly less when together.

concept: a sound get quieter as you get farther away
analogy: Imagine I give you a baseball and some plasticine and ask you to cover the ball with the plasticine.  Once you've done that, I take all the plasticine you used and ask you to cover a ball twice as big. You'll still be able to do it, but you'll really have to make the plasticine thin. That's because the same amount of plasticine has to cover more area. Sound works the same way. A sound goes out equally in all directions, so the farther you are away from the source of the sound, the less energy (loudness) there will be.  In the same way the plasticine gets thinner, the sound gets quieter.

concept: a wireless phone charger work

So think of it like this - the plug in the wall brings the electricity to the charging base, which changes it in to a magnetic field. The receiver in your phone converts this magnetic field back to electricity which it uses to charge your phone.

concept: Can someone explain the specific mechanism for how we develop a resistance to th
analogy: You, personally, don't become resistant to antibiotics. The microbes you try to kill with antibiotics do. Some of the bacteria inside you have mutations in their genome. Let's say that some are more resistant to heat and some to penicillin. The heat resistance doesn't matter. Your temperature will never ever get above 42 degrees Celsius, so those bacteria live, reproduce and die just like the rest. Now, if you're hit with a large dose of penicillin, every other bacteria *but* the penicillin-resistant ones die. Suddenly there's zero competition for the resistant ones, and they start reproducing quickly.

concept: voyager spacecraft sent images of Uranus and Neptune

It doesn't create any more value, it just creates more money, but each piece is worth a little bit less than it used to be.

concept: Can someone please explain the Chaos Theory LI5 please
analogy: Let's say you have a ball on a smooth floor. You roll it away from you in some direction and watch where it ends up. Now, you start again, rolling it *almost* the same direction with *almost* the same force. It should end up at just about the same place it ended up the first time. That is, *unless* it hits a rough patch in the floor, or the ball isn't perfectly spherical, or any number of minor variations in the environment conspire to change where it ends up. In that case, the small change in your starting conditions (starting place, initial force, et cetera) can produce *very* different results in where the ball ends up. This sensitivity of the outcome to starting conditions is called chaos, and chaos theory is the branch of mathematics that deals with describing chaotic systems.

concept: The difference between pixel- and vector-based images
analogy: Raster images (those made up of pixels) have a fixed size. It's like taking a piece of graph paper and coloring in the squares. If you want to make a picture bigger, you only have as much information as was in the original pixels. This makes the image blocky or fuzzy when you zoom in or try to blow it up. Vector images, OTOH, are made up of lines and curves that are mathematically defined. If you ever want to change the size, the vector engine recomputes things. You can scale vector images 'perfectly' at any scale. Raster images are easier to display & better at capturing photographic detail. Vector images can be significantly more work to create.

concept: Difference between Special and General Relativity
analogy: Special relativity describes flat space; general relativity describes curved space. Note that general relativity is a superset of special. It's not an evolutionary step - GR doesn't replace SR like GR replaced Newton. On a scale where the curvature of space is approximately zero, things behave just like SR says.  Note that all other forces and particles inhabit space, so SR is really a statement about the geometry that applies to all forces and particles. Although it was first discovered in the context of electromagnetism, it's not unique to electromagnetism.

concept: What's the difference between a flash drive and external hard drive
analogy: An external hard disk is called as such because it utilizes a Hard Disk Drive (HDD). One of these drives has a metal platter with different magnetic sections, called sectors, that are read by a small magnetic sensor called a head on the end of an actuator arm. Many people are now moving over to flash storage, however, which is what a flash drive utilizes and thus is named for. Flash storage does not contain moving parts but rather is similar to a large grid of capacitors and transistors that are used to keep data. It is faster to access than an HDD because you do not need to wait for the disk to spin to the correct location. It can decay, however, unlike an HDD. The more an area of flash is written the more it wears out and eventually it will fail.

concept: the 3D effect created in movies
analogy: You need a second camera that is filming from a slightly different perspective, just like humans can see in three dimensions because they have two eyes set slightly apart. (In computer animation you can arbitrarily create a second perspective on a scene, so it's easier but it still requires extra rendering to produce the film.) You play back footage from both perspectives at the same time, but the viewer wears polarized lenses which filter the image so that the left eye sees one thing and the right eye another. You may recall older 3D using colored lenses for the same purpose. You can also process a standard film to give it computer-generated 3D effects. This is a lot cheaper than filming a real 3D movie, but the result tends to be ugly.
